import Callout from '../../../components/Callout.astro';

Two companies become one. Either they pool everything and carry on as if they had always been
a single business, or one of them simply buys the other's business and accounts for it like any
purchase. AS 14 exists to keep those two stories from being told with the same journal entries —
because the first preserves reserves and book values, and the second creates goodwill or capital
reserve and drops the seller's reserves entirely.

This chapter is the biggest single-numerical scorer in Module 3: a full question walks you from
purchase consideration through the transferee's incorporation entries to the post-amalgamation
balance sheet. Every mark sits inside a working note, and every working note comes from a small
set of fixed rules. This page gives you the rules, the three standard computations, and the traps.

## 1. Who is who, and the three flavours

**Amalgamation** under AS 14 means an amalgamation pursuant to the Companies Act 2013 or any
other applicable statute. The company that dies is the **transferor** (vendor) company; the
company that receives its business is the **transferee** (purchasing) company. Exam stems use
all four labels interchangeably — fix the direction before touching a number.

Three arrangements wear the same accounting clothes:

- **Amalgamation (strict sense):** A Ltd and B Ltd combine into a newly formed C Ltd. Both
  transferors vanish; the new company is the transferee.
- **Absorption:** A Ltd continues and takes over B Ltd. No new company is formed; B Ltd is the
  transferor.
- **External reconstruction:** a new company is formed for the specific purpose of taking over
  the business of one existing (often struggling) company, which is then wound up. One seller,
  one newly incorporated buyer — the accounting is identical to amalgamation.

AS 14 governs all three. What it does **not** govern here: acquisitions where the acquired
company stays alive as a subsidiary (that is consolidation territory), and Ind AS 103 — the
Inter P1 syllabus tests AS 14, not Ind AS.

## 2. Merger or purchase: the five conditions

AS 14 recognises exactly two species. An **amalgamation in the nature of merger** must satisfy
**all five** conditions of para 3(e); failing even one makes it an **amalgamation in the nature
of purchase**.

| # | Condition (para 3(e)) | The idea |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | All assets **and** all liabilities of the transferor become assets and liabilities of the transferee | Nothing is left behind |
| 2 | Holders of at least **90% (by face value) of the transferor's equity shares** — not counting shares the transferee or its subsidiaries/nominees already hold — must end up as equity shareholders of the transferee | Continuity of ownership |
| 3 | Those equity shareholders are paid **wholly in equity shares** of the transferee — cash allowed only for **fractional entitlements** | No cash-out of the owners |
| 4 | The transferee **intends to carry on** the transferor's business | Continuity of business |
| 5 | Book values of assets and liabilities are carried forward **unchanged**, except adjustments needed to bring **accounting policies into line** (e.g. re-stating inventory from weighted average to FIFO) | Continuity of values |

<Callout kind="mistake">
  <p>
    <strong>The 90% test is on FACE VALUE of equity shares, not on votes, not on all
    securities, and not on the whole register.</strong> Shares the transferee (or its
    subsidiaries or nominees) already holds are excluded from the base before you test 90%.
    A stem where the transferee already owns 10,000 of the transferor's 2,00,000 shares asks
    for 90% of 1,90,000 — that is 1,71,000 shares, not 1,80,000.
  </p>
</Callout>

Why anyone cares: a merger uses the **pooling of interests method** (reserves survive, no
goodwill), a purchase uses the **purchase method** (reserves die, goodwill or capital reserve
is born). The classification drives every entry that follows.

## 3. Purchase consideration

Para 3(g) of AS 14 defines **purchase consideration** as the aggregate of shares and other
securities issued, plus cash or other assets paid, by the transferee **to the shareholders of
the transferor** — equity and preference shareholders both.

<Callout kind="pointer">
  <p>
    <strong>Only payments to shareholders count.</strong> Whatever the transferee pays or
    promises to debenture holders or trade creditors of the transferor is NOT purchase
    consideration — a debenture liability taken over simply enters the net-assets computation
    as a liability assumed. Liquidation expenses the transferee reimburses are not purchase
    consideration either; they are debited to goodwill (or capital reserve). This single rule
    kills more marks in this chapter than any other.
  </p>
</Callout>

Four standard ways a stem hands you the number:

1. **Lump sum** — stated outright; nothing to compute.
2. **Net payment** — add up everything given to equity and preference shareholders: shares
   issued (at issue price), cash, other assets. Ignore payments to anyone else.
3. **Net assets** — assets taken over at **agreed values** minus liabilities assumed
   (outside liabilities only — never share capital or reserves). Book values apply only
   where no value was agreed.
4. **Intrinsic value / share exchange** — value each company's share from its net assets
   (intrinsic value = net assets ÷ number of equity shares), set the exchange ratio, then
   purchase consideration = shares issued × issue value, plus any cash for fractions.

### Worked Example 1 — net payment method

Narmada Chemicals Ltd takes over Tapti Dyes Ltd. Tapti has 1,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 and
5,000 preference shares of ₹100. Terms: preference shareholders are paid cash at a 5% premium;
equity shareholders receive 3 equity shares of Narmada (₹10 each, agreed issue value ₹15) for
every 4 shares held, plus ₹2 cash per share held. Narmada also agrees to discharge Tapti's
₹4,00,000 debentures at par.

**Working Note 1 — preference shareholders.** 5,000 × ₹105 = **₹5,25,000** cash.

**Working Note 2 — equity shareholders.** Shares issued = 1,00,000 × 3/4 = 75,000 shares at
₹15 = ₹11,25,000. Cash = 1,00,000 × ₹2 = ₹2,00,000. Subtotal ₹13,25,000.

**Working Note 3 — purchase consideration.** 5,25,000 + 13,25,000 = **₹18,50,000**. The
₹4,00,000 to debenture holders is excluded — it is a liability taken over, not consideration
to shareholders.

## 4. The two accounting methods

### Pooling of interests (merger only)

- Assets, liabilities **and reserves** of the transferor come in at their **existing carrying
  amounts** (adjusted only for uniform accounting policies).
- The identity of reserves is preserved — general reserve stays general reserve, P&L balance
  stays P&L balance.
- The only plug: the difference between the **share capital issued** (plus any cash or other
  consideration) and the transferor's **share capital** is adjusted **in reserves**. Issue more
  capital than you cancel, and reserves shrink by the difference; issue less, and reserves grow.

The three-step working note: **Step 1** — consideration recorded (capital issued + cash etc.).
**Step 2** — transferor's paid-up capital (equity + preference). **Step 3** — Step 1 − Step 2,
adjusted against reserves.

### Purchase method (everything else)

- Assets and liabilities come in either at existing carrying amounts or at the **fair values**
  the consideration was allocated over — as the terms provide. Reserves of the transferor
  (other than statutory reserves — next section) are **not recorded at all**.
- Compare **net assets taken over** with **purchase consideration**:
  - Consideration **higher** → the excess is **goodwill**.
  - Net assets **higher** → the shortfall is **capital reserve**.
- **Goodwill arising on amalgamation is amortised over a period not exceeding five years**,
  unless a somewhat longer period can be justified.

<Callout kind="mistake">
  <p>
    <strong>Direction check before you write "goodwill".</strong> Paying ₹27,50,000 for net
    assets of ₹25,00,000 gives goodwill of ₹2,50,000. Getting net assets of ₹30,00,000 for
    ₹27,50,000 gives capital reserve. Goodwill = you paid MORE than you got. If your goodwill
    figure appears when the consideration is the smaller number, the working note is upside
    down.
  </p>
</Callout>

### Worked Example 2 — purchase method with goodwill

Godavari Foods Ltd absorbs Krishna Agro Ltd (an amalgamation in the nature of purchase) for a
purchase consideration of ₹27,50,000. Agreed values of assets taken over: PPE ₹24,00,000,
inventory ₹6,50,000, trade receivables ₹5,50,000, cash ₹1,00,000. Liabilities assumed: 12%
debentures ₹8,00,000 and trade creditors ₹4,00,000. Krishna's books also show a general
reserve of ₹3,00,000 and an investment allowance reserve (statutory) of ₹1,50,000.

**Working Note 1 — net assets taken over.** Assets 24,00,000 + 6,50,000 + 5,50,000 + 1,00,000
= ₹37,00,000. Less liabilities 8,00,000 + 4,00,000 = ₹12,00,000. Net assets = **₹25,00,000**.

**Working Note 2 — goodwill.** Consideration 27,50,000 − net assets 25,00,000 =
**goodwill ₹2,50,000**, to be amortised over not more than five years — ₹50,000 a year on a
straight-line basis unless a longer period is justified.

**Working Note 3 — reserves.** The general reserve of ₹3,00,000 is ignored (purchase method).
The statutory reserve of ₹1,50,000 must be kept alive — see the next section for the entry.

### Worked Example 3 — pooling of interests

Sabarmati Textiles Ltd and Mahi Mills Ltd amalgamate in the nature of merger. Mahi's balance
sheet: equity capital ₹16,00,000 (1,60,000 shares of ₹10), general reserve ₹6,00,000, P&L
balance ₹2,40,000. Sabarmati (general reserve ₹10,00,000) issues 1,90,000 equity shares of ₹10
to Mahi's shareholders. All assets and liabilities come across at book value.

**Working Note — reserve adjustment.** Capital issued = ₹19,00,000. Mahi's capital =
₹16,00,000. Excess = ₹3,00,000, deducted from reserves. Combined general reserve = 10,00,000
+ 6,00,000 − 3,00,000 = **₹13,00,000**; Mahi's P&L balance of ₹2,40,000 carries forward with
its identity intact. No goodwill, no capital reserve — pooling never creates either.

## 5. Statutory reserves and the Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve

Some reserves exist because a statute demanded them (investment allowance reserve, export
profit reserve, and similar). In a **purchase**, they are the one exception to the
"transferor's reserves die" rule: while the statute still requires the reserve to be
maintained, the transferee records it with this entry:

| Journal | Dr | Cr |
|---|---|---|
| Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve A/c Dr | ✓ | |
| — To Statutory Reserve A/c | | ✓ |

The **Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve** appears as a **separate line item** shown as a
*negative* balance under Other Equity / Reserves — it is **not** netted off against the
statutory reserve it mirrors. When the statutory holding period ends, the entry is simply
reversed and both lines disappear.

<Callout kind="pointer">
  <p>
    <strong>"Reserve", not "Account".</strong> Older material calls this the Amalgamation
    Adjustment <em>Account</em>; an MCA amendment in 2016 renamed it Amalgamation Adjustment
    <em>Reserve</em> and fixed its presentation as a separate (negative) head within reserves.
    An MCQ option offering the old name with set-off against statutory reserve is a planted
    trap. In a merger this machinery is never needed — pooling carries ALL reserves across
    anyway.
  </p>
</Callout>

## 6. Closing the transferor's books

The transferor dies by ledger. The sequence — every step its own working note:

1. **Open Realisation A/c** and transfer all assets taken over at **book values** (including
   goodwill if on the books; cash too, if taken over).
2. Transfer **liabilities taken over** at book values to the credit side.
3. **Consideration due:** `Transferee Co. A/c Dr → To Realisation A/c` with the purchase
   consideration.
4. Liabilities **not** taken over (say, debentures the transferee refused) are settled by the
   transferor directly; any difference from book value lands in Realisation A/c.
5. **Liquidation expenses:** borne by the transferor → debit Realisation A/c. Reimbursed or
   borne by the transferee → the transferor's books ignore them (the transferee debits
   goodwill/capital reserve).
6. Balance of Realisation A/c = **profit or loss on realisation** → transferred to the
   **Equity Shareholders A/c**.
7. Settle preference shareholders (premium/discount versus book value routes through
   Realisation A/c), then distribute shares and cash received to equity shareholders, closing
   the Equity Shareholders A/c to nil.

The Equity Shareholders A/c is the final scorecard: capital + reserves + realisation profit −
losses − what they receive = nil. If it doesn't close, a working note upstream is wrong.

## 7. In the transferee's books, and the inter-company clean-ups

The standard incorporation sequence (purchase method):

| Journal | What it does |
|---|---|
| Business Purchase A/c Dr → To Liquidator of Transferor Co. | Consideration becomes payable |
| Assets taken over Dr → To Liabilities assumed; To Business Purchase; (balance) To Capital Reserve — or Goodwill Dr | Assets/liabilities incorporated at agreed values; goodwill or capital reserve plugs |
| Liquidator of Transferor Co. Dr → To Equity Share Capital / Securities Premium / Bank | Consideration discharged (shares at issue price split into capital and premium) |
| Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve Dr → To Statutory Reserve | Only if statutory reserves must survive |

Under pooling, the same skeleton runs at carrying amounts, reserves are brought in with their
identity, and the capital-vs-capital difference adjusts reserves instead of creating goodwill.

Then two clean-ups the examiner loves:

- **Mutual owings:** if the companies owed each other money, the combined books would show a
  receivable and a payable that no longer face an outsider. Eliminate after the acquisition
  entries: `Trade Payables Dr → To Trade Receivables` for the mutual amount.
- **Unrealised profit in stock:** if one company holds inventory bought from the other, the
  seller's profit margin inside that stock is unrealised for the combined entity. Reduce the
  stock to its cost to the group; the matching debit goes to goodwill/capital reserve under
  the purchase method (or against revenue reserves in a merger).

<Callout kind="example" label="Working-note discipline = marks">
  <p>
    A full amalgamation answer is five working notes in a fixed order: (1) purchase
    consideration, (2) net assets / reserve adjustment, (3) goodwill or capital reserve,
    (4) statutory reserve entry, (5) inter-company eliminations — then the balance sheet.
    Label each note, state the method you concluded (merger/purchase) and WHY in one line,
    and the marks follow the trail.
  </p>
</Callout>

That's the chapter. Classify with the five conditions (§2), price with the four consideration
methods (§3, WE 1), plug with goodwill or the reserve adjustment (§4, WE 2–3), keep statutory
reserves alive through the Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve (§5), and finish with the mutual
owings and stock-profit eliminations (§7).
